answer:
I think it really depends on the situation whether most people are trustworthy or not. In general, I'm inclined to give people the benefit of the doubt and assume they have good intentions unless they give me a reason not to trust them. But I also try to be smart and cautious in certain contexts where people may try to take advantage. 

For example, when I'm dealing with strangers, especially in places like big cities, I keep my guard up more because some people out there will try to scam you if you're too naive. If some random person on the street asks me for money, I usually don't just hand over cash because they could easily be lying about their situation. However, if I'm interacting with neighbors I've known for years or co-workers I've built relationships with, I'm much more likely to trust them because they've demonstrated over time that they're honest, reliable people.

I do think there are still a lot of good, caring people out there who aim to do the right thing. But shady characters are also looking to take advantage of others' trust and naivety. So, while I try not to become cynical, I've learned through experience to balance openness with measured caution when trusting others. At the end of the day, I feel it's important to give people a chance while also protecting myself until they've earned my trust. Discernment, empathy, and wisdom help me navigate when and how much to trust people in different contexts.
title: Do you think that most people are trustworthy, or do you think that most people are untrustworthy? Explain your reasoning.
